title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Holding Your Breath: The World of Competitive Freediving
content.text: At the surface of a sun-lit ocean near the Bahamas, freediver Alessia Zecchini takes a final breath, tucks her body into a vertical position and descends. There is no scuba tank, no regulator, no mechanical assistance of any kind. She will reach a depth of over one hundred metres on a single breath of air before returning, alone, to the surface. (37) [37] El apnea competitivo lleva el cuerpo humano a sus límites extremos. [/37] For the small but passionate global community that practises competitive freediving, this kind of achievement represents the ultimate expression of what the human body can endure. || The physiological adaptations that allow freedivers to descend so deep are extraordinary. As a diver goes deeper, the pressure of the water compresses the air in the lungs dramatically. (38) [38] La fisiología de la inmersión profunda es fascinante y compleja. [/38] At one hundred metres, the pressure is eleven times that at the surface, compressing the lungs to roughly the size of a fist. The body compensates with a suite of reflexes known collectively as the mammalian dive response, which slows the heart rate and redistributes blood flow to protect vital organs. || Training for competitive freediving is as much mental as physical. Athletes practise breath-holding in swimming pools, building their tolerance for the mounting urge to breathe that is caused not by a lack of oxygen but by a build-up of carbon dioxide. (39) [39] La tolerancia al CO2 es una habilidad clave que se entrena. [/39] Yoga, meditation and relaxation techniques are used alongside physical conditioning to help divers remain calm under the intense pressure of depth. || The sport is governed by international body AIDA, which recognises multiple competitive disciplines. Static apnoea tests how long a diver can hold their breath while floating motionless at the surface. (40) [40] Las distintas modalidades ponen a prueba capacidades diferentes. [/40] Dynamic apnoea and the various depth disciplines test different combinations of distance, time and depth in conditions that push human physiology to its limits. || Safety is a paramount concern in competition. All competitive dives must be performed with trained safety divers stationed at depth, ready to intervene if a competitor loses consciousness. (41) [41] La seguridad en la apnea competitiva depende de un equipo coordinado. [/41] Shallow-water blackout — a sudden loss of consciousness caused by oxygen depletion near the surface — is the sport's most serious risk, and its prevention requires constant vigilance from both athletes and safety teams. || Despite the risks, freediving is growing in popularity, attracting practitioners who value its simplicity, the silence of the underwater world and the profound self-knowledge that comes from testing one's limits. (42) [42] La apnea atrae a quienes buscan una conexión profunda consigo mismos y con el mar. [/42] 'When you are at one hundred metres, breathing is an irrelevance,' says one champion. 'All that exists is the water and you.'

paragraphs.A: This phenomenon can occur without warning, which is why no freediver should ever train alone.
paragraphs.B: Equalising the pressure in the ears and sinuses is an additional technical skill that must be mastered before any serious depth training can begin.
paragraphs.C: Each event rewards a distinct physical and psychological quality in the athlete.
paragraphs.D: Freedivers describe the experience as a kind of moving meditation, a chance to find stillness at the edge of the body's capabilities.
paragraphs.E: Learning to extend this threshold without triggering panic is one of the most demanding aspects of the discipline.
paragraphs.F: The world records being set today would have seemed biologically impossible to sports scientists just thirty years ago.
paragraphs.G: Without their presence and rapid response, the competitive freediving circuit could not safely operate.

questions.explanation: F places current achievements in historical perspective, reinforcing the idea that freediving pushes the human body to extreme limits.
B adds pressure equalisation as an additional technical skill that completes the description of the complex physiology of diving.
E describes the training of CO2 tolerance and the difficulty of extending that threshold without panicking.
C summarises that each modality tests a different quality before the text describes dynamic apnea and depth disciplines.
G describes the indispensable function of safety divers and why without them the circuit could not function.
D captures the meditative and introspective motivation of practitioners, preparing the final quote from the champion about the silence at a hundred metres.
